---
title: "Particle MCMC"
format: html
engine: julia
order: 7
---

# Introduction

The aim of this session is to learn how you can fit a stochastic model using the particle MCMC Metropolis-Hastings algorithm (pMCMC).
As you saw in the lecture, fitting a model with a likelihood approach requires you to explore the parameter space efficiently and to evaluate the likelihood at a given parameter.

In the previous session on MCMC, you have used Metropolis-Hastings to explore the parameter space and evaluated the likelihood for deterministic models.
In order to fit a stochastic model, we can still use MH to explore the parameter space efficiently but we need to use a new method to evaluate the likelihood.
This new method is called a particle filter.

# Objectives

In this session you will:

1. Understand how particle filters estimate likelihoods for stochastic models
2. Fit the stochastic SEIT4L model to the Tristan da Cunha outbreak using Turing.jl
3. Compare stochastic vs deterministic model fits
4. Learn about calibrating particle filters

::: {.callout-note collapse="true"}

# Setup

```{julia}
#| echo: false
#| include: false

using Pkg
# Activate the project root (one level up from sessions/)
project_root = joinpath(@__DIR__, "..")
Pkg.activate(project_root)
```

## Source file

The source file of this session is located at `sessions/pmcmc.qmd`.

## Load libraries

```{julia}
using Random ## for random numbers
using Distributions ## for probability distributions
using DifferentialEquations ## for differential equations
using JumpProcesses ## for stochastic simulation (Gillespie algorithm)
using DataFrames ## for data frames
using Plots ## for plots
using StatsPlots ## for statistical plots
using Turing ## for probabilistic programming and MCMC
using MCMCChains ## for MCMC diagnostics
using AdvancedPS ## for particle filtering
using CSV ## for CSV file reading
using StatsBase ## for statistical functions
```

## Initialisation

We set a random seed for reproducibility.
Setting this ensures that you should get exactly the same results on your computer as we do.

```{julia}
Random.seed!(1234)
```

:::

# The SEIT4L stochastic model

In the previous session, you defined Turing `@model` functions for SEITL and SEIT4L that work for both simulation and inference.
In this session, we'll use the same pattern with SEIT4L to demonstrate particle MCMC.

First, let's define the SEIT4L stochastic model using the Gillespie algorithm.
Julia's `JumpProcesses.jl` (part of `DifferentialEquations.jl`) provides excellent support for stochastic simulation:

```{julia}
"""
SEIT4L stochastic model using Gillespie algorithm.
"""
function simulate_seit4l_stochastic(θ, init_state, times)
    # Parameters
    R_0, D_lat, D_inf, α, D_imm = θ[:R_0], θ[:D_lat], θ[:D_inf], θ[:α], θ[:D_imm]
    β = R_0 / D_inf
    ϵ = 1.0 / D_lat
    ν = 1.0 / D_inf
    τ = 4.0 / D_imm  # Rate for each of 4 T stages

    # Initial state
    u0 = [init_state[:S], init_state[:E], init_state[:I],
          init_state[:T1], init_state[:T2], init_state[:T3], init_state[:T4],
          init_state[:L], 0.0]

    # Rate function
    function rate_func(u, p, t)
        S, E, I, T1, T2, T3, T4, L, Inc = u
        N = S + E + I + T1 + T2 + T3 + T4 + L

        return [
            β * S * I / N,           # S → E (infection)
            ϵ * E,                   # E → I (becoming infectious)
            ν * I,                   # I → T1 (recovery to temporary immunity stage 1)
            τ * T1,                  # T1 → T2
            τ * T2,                  # T2 → T3
            τ * T3,                  # T3 → T4
            (1 - α) * τ * T4,        # T4 → S (loss of immunity)
            α * τ * T4               # T4 → L (gain long-term immunity)
        ]
    end

    # Stoichiometry matrix
    stoich = [
        -1  1  0  0  0  0  0  0  0;  # S → E
         0 -1  1  0  0  0  0  0  1;  # E → I (also increments Inc)
         0  0 -1  1  0  0  0  0  0;  # I → T1
         0  0  0 -1  1  0  0  0  0;  # T1 → T2
         0  0  0  0 -1  1  0  0  0;  # T2 → T3
         0  0  0  0  0 -1  1  0  0;  # T3 → T4
         1  0  0  0  0  0 -1  0  0;  # T4 → S
         0  0  0  0  0  0 -1  1  0   # T4 → L
    ]

    # Define jump problem
    prob = DiscreteProblem(u0, (times[1], times[end]))

    # Create jump processes
    # We need to create separate affect! functions for each jump to avoid closure issues
    jumps = ConstantRateJump[]
    for i in 1:size(stoich, 1)
        # Capture the stoichiometry row for this specific jump
        stoich_row = stoich[i, :]
        affect! = function(integrator)
            for j in 1:length(integrator.u)
                integrator.u[j] += stoich_row[j]
            end
        end
        rate(u, p, t) = rate_func(u, p, t)[i]
        push!(jumps, ConstantRateJump(rate, affect!))
    end

    jump_prob = JumpProblem(prob, Direct(), jumps...)
    sol = solve(jump_prob, SSAStepper())

    # Extract solution at requested time points
    # sol(t) interpolates the solution at time t
    state_names = [:S, :E, :I, :T1, :T2, :T3, :T4, :L, :Inc_cumulative]

    # Evaluate solution at requested times
    sol_at_times = [sol(t) for t in times]
    sol_matrix = reduce(hcat, sol_at_times)'
    df = DataFrame(sol_matrix, state_names)
    df[!, :time] = collect(times)

    # Convert cumulative incidence to daily incidence
    df[!, :Inc] = [0.0; diff(df.Inc_cumulative)]

    return df
end
```

Let's visualize a few stochastic realizations to see the variability:

```{julia}
# Load Tristan da Cunha data using the datadir() function from the DrWatson package
using CSV, DrWatson
# Alternatively, you can load directly from GitHub:
# flu_tdc_url = "https://raw.githubusercontent.com/sbfnk/mfiidd.julia/main/data/flu_tdc_1971.csv"
# flu_tdc = CSV.read(download(flu_tdc_url), DataFrame)
flu_tdc = CSV.read(datadir("flu_tdc_1971.csv"), DataFrame)

# Parameters (reasonable values from literature)
θ = Dict(
    :R_0 => 7.0,
    :D_lat => 1.0,
    :D_inf => 4.0,
    :α => 0.5,
    :D_imm => 10.0,
    :ρ => 0.65
)

# Initial state for SEIT4L
init_state = Dict(
    :S => 279.0,
    :E => 0.0,
    :I => 2.0,
    :T1 => 3.0,
    :T2 => 0.0,
    :T3 => 0.0,
    :T4 => 0.0,
    :L => 0.0
)

# Plot multiple realizations
p = plot(title = "SEIT4L Stochastic Realizations",
         xlabel = "Time (days)", ylabel = "Daily incidence")

for i in 1:20
    traj = simulate_seit4l_stochastic(θ, init_state, 0.0:1.0:59.0)
    plot!(p, traj.time, traj.Inc, alpha = 0.3, color = :lightblue, label = "")
end

scatter!(p, flu_tdc.time, flu_tdc.obs,
         label = "Observed data", markersize = 4, color = :red)

plot!(p)
```

Notice the high variability between realizations - this is demographic stochasticity in action.

# Particle MCMC with Turing.jl

Turing.jl provides built-in support for particle MCMC through the `AdvancedPS.jl` package.
We will use the Particle Gibbs (PG) sampler, which jointly samples parameters and state trajectories.
This is particularly useful for models like SEIT4L with high-dimensional state spaces (9 state variables over 60 time points) as it avoids particle degeneracy by maintaining a reference trajectory.
An alternative is Particle Marginal Metropolis-Hastings (PMMH), which uses a bootstrap particle filter but can suffer from degeneracy when particles have negligible weight.

To define a model for particle MCMC in Turing.jl, we specify the priors and the stochastic simulation just as we would for a deterministic model.
The Particle Gibbs sampler then handles the particle filtering automatically:

```{julia}
"""
Turing model for pMCMC inference of SEIT4L parameters using Particle Gibbs.

For stochastic models, we define the model with observations and let Turing's
Particle Gibbs sampler handle the particle filtering automatically.
"""
@model function pmcmc_seit4l(obs, init_state)
    # Priors
    R_0 ~ Uniform(1.0, 50.0)
    D_lat ~ Uniform(0.1, 10.0)
    D_inf ~ Uniform(0.1, 15.0)
    α ~ Uniform(0.0, 1.0)
    D_imm ~ Uniform(1.0, 50.0)
    ρ ~ Uniform(0.0, 1.0)

    # Create parameter dictionary
    θ = Dict(:R_0 => R_0, :D_lat => D_lat, :D_inf => D_inf,
             :α => α, :D_imm => D_imm, :ρ => ρ)

    # Simulate one trajectory (Particle Gibbs will run this many times)
    times = 0.0:1.0:59.0
    traj = simulate_seit4l_stochastic(θ, init_state, times)

    # Likelihood: observed data given simulated incidence
    for i in eachindex(obs)
        λ = max(ρ * traj.Inc[i], 1e-10)
        obs[i] ~ Poisson(λ)
    end
end
```

Now let's run pMCMC using Turing's Particle Gibbs sampler:

```{julia}
# Define the model - pass observations vector directly
model_pmcmc = pmcmc_seit4l(flu_tdc.obs, init_state)

# Run Particle MCMC with Particle Gibbs sampler
# PG(n_particles) where n_particles is the number of particles for the filter
using AdvancedPS

# Run with 20 particles for demonstration (use 50-100 for production)
chain_pmcmc = sample(model_pmcmc, PG(20), 1000, progress=false)
```

::: {.callout-tip collapse="true"}
## Code explainer
- [`PG(n_particles)`](https://github.com/TuringLang/AdvancedPS.jl) - Particle Gibbs sampler from `AdvancedPS.jl`. Uses `n_particles` parallel particles to estimate the likelihood at each MCMC iteration. More particles give more accurate estimates but cost more computation. Typical values: 20-50 for exploration, 50-100 for production.
:::

```{julia}
# Display chain summary
display(chain_pmcmc)
```

```{julia}
# Plot trace plots
plot(chain_pmcmc)
```

```{julia}
# Plot posterior densities
density(chain_pmcmc, size = (800, 600))
```

::: {.callout-warning}

Running pMCMC is computationally expensive! The Particle Gibbs sampler needs to run multiple stochastic simulations at each MCMC iteration.
With 1000 MCMC iterations and 20 particles, this means 20,000+ stochastic model simulations.

For production use, you should run multiple chains in parallel using `MCMCThreads()` (e.g. `sample(model, PG(50), MCMCThreads(), 2000, 4)`), use more particles (50-100) for better likelihood estimates, and run longer chains (5,000-10,000+ iterations) for convergence.
Fortunately, Particle Gibbs adapts automatically, so there's no need for manual tuning.

:::

# Comparing with deterministic model fits

Let's quickly fit the deterministic SEIT4L model for comparison:

```{julia}
"""
SEIT4L deterministic model.
"""
function seit4l_ode!(du, u, p, t)
    R_0, D_lat, D_inf, α, D_imm = p
    β = R_0 / D_inf
    ϵ = 1.0 / D_lat
    ν = 1.0 / D_inf
    τ = 4.0 / D_imm

    S, E, I, T1, T2, T3, T4, L, Inc = u
    N = S + E + I + T1 + T2 + T3 + T4 + L

    du[1] = -β * S * I / N + (1 - α) * τ * T4
    du[2] = β * S * I / N - ϵ * E
    du[3] = ϵ * E - ν * I
    du[4] = ν * I - τ * T1
    du[5] = τ * T1 - τ * T2
    du[6] = τ * T2 - τ * T3
    du[7] = τ * T3 - τ * T4
    du[8] = α * τ * T4
    du[9] = ϵ * E
end

@model function seit4l_deterministic_model(obs, init_state, times)
    # Priors
    R_0 ~ Uniform(1.0, 50.0)
    D_lat ~ Uniform(0.1, 10.0)
    D_inf ~ Uniform(0.1, 15.0)
    α ~ Uniform(0.0, 1.0)
    D_imm ~ Uniform(1.0, 50.0)
    ρ ~ Uniform(0.0, 1.0)

    # Solve ODE
    params = [R_0, D_lat, D_inf, α, D_imm]
    u0 = [init_state[:S], init_state[:E], init_state[:I],
          init_state[:T1], init_state[:T2], init_state[:T3], init_state[:T4],
          init_state[:L], 0.0]

    prob = ODEProblem(seit4l_ode!, u0, (times[1], times[end]), params)
    sol = solve(prob, Tsit5(), saveat=times)

    # Extract daily incidence
    inc_cumulative = [sol[i][9] for i in 1:length(sol)]
    inc_daily = [0.0; diff(inc_cumulative)]

    # Likelihood: Poisson observations
    # obs[i] corresponds to inc_daily[i+1] (time i, since inc_daily[1] is at time 0)
    for i in 1:length(obs)
        # obs has 60 elements (times 0-59), inc_daily has 60 elements (indices 1-60)
        # obs[i] is at time i-1, so it corresponds to inc_daily[i]
        λ = max(ρ * inc_daily[i], 1e-10)
        obs[i] ~ Poisson(λ)
    end
end
```

```{julia}
# Fit deterministic model
times_obs = 0.0:1.0:59.0
model_det = seit4l_deterministic_model(flu_tdc.obs, init_state, times_obs)
chain_det = sample(model_det, NUTS(), 2000, progress=false)
```

```{julia}
# Compare posteriors
p1 = density(chain_det, :R_0, title = "R_0 (Deterministic)", legend = false)
p2 = density(chain_pmcmc, :R_0, title = "R_0 (Stochastic)", legend = false)
p3 = density(chain_det, :α, title = "α (Deterministic)", legend = false)
p4 = density(chain_pmcmc, :α, title = "α (Stochastic)", legend = false)

plot(p1, p2, p3, p4, layout = (2, 2), size = (800, 600))
```

__What differences do you notice between the deterministic and stochastic fits?__

For the Tristan da Cunha outbreak with only 284 individuals, demographic stochasticity plays a significant role.
In practice, the stochastic model typically shows wider posterior distributions (more uncertainty), different point estimates for some parameters, and better capture of the irregularity in the data.
However, the deterministic model is much faster to fit (NUTS vs particle filter) and often provides similar qualitative insights.

::: {.callout-note title="How particle filters work"}

A particle filter estimates the likelihood $P(\text{data}|\theta)$ for a stochastic model by simulating multiple trajectories (particles) through the system.
At each observation time:

1. Particles are propagated forward using the stochastic model
2. Each particle is weighted according to how well it matches the observed data
3. Particles with low weights are discarded and replaced by resampling from high-weight particles

Particles represent the distribution of possible trajectories that could have generated the observed data, given the parameters.
The likelihood is computed from the particle weights across all observation times.

Turing.jl's `AdvancedPS.jl` package implements sophisticated particle filtering algorithms (including Particle Gibbs) automatically, so you don't need to code your own.

:::

# Choosing the number of particles

A key question in particle MCMC is: how many particles do we need? The answer involves a trade-off. Too few particles produce noisy likelihood estimates, which leads to poor MCMC mixing and unreliable inference. Too many particles waste computation without improving results.

The goal is to find the minimum number of particles that gives stable likelihood estimates. In practice, this means starting with a small number (10-20 particles) for initial exploratory runs to check that the model works and the sampler can find reasonable parameter values. Once you're confident the model is correctly specified, you can examine the trace plots: if they look very noisy or the chain gets stuck frequently, increase the particle count. For final inference that you plan to report or publish, using 50-100 particles provides a good balance between accuracy and computation time.

A useful diagnostic is to monitor the variance in log-likelihood estimates across iterations. This variance should decrease as you add particles, eventually plateauing when you have enough. For a dataset like Tristan da Cunha with 60 observations and 284 individuals, 20-50 particles is typically sufficient.

# Model comparison: SEITL vs SEIT4L

In the [SEITL session](seitl.qmd), we introduced two model variants that both explain the two-wave pattern through temporary immunity:

- **SEITL**: Single T compartment with exponential duration (memoryless)
- **SEIT4L**: Four T sub-compartments giving Erlang-4 duration (more realistic timing)

Which model better explains the data? Let's fit both and compare.

## Define the SEITL stochastic model

First, we need the simpler SEITL model for comparison:

```{julia}
"""
SEITL stochastic model using Gillespie algorithm.
Single T compartment (exponential duration).
"""
function simulate_seitl_stochastic(θ, init_state, times)
    R_0, D_lat, D_inf, α, D_imm = θ[:R_0], θ[:D_lat], θ[:D_inf], θ[:α], θ[:D_imm]
    β = R_0 / D_inf
    ϵ = 1.0 / D_lat
    ν = 1.0 / D_inf
    τ = 1.0 / D_imm  # Single T compartment

    # Initial state: S, E, I, T, L, Inc_cumulative
    u0 = [init_state[:S], init_state[:E], init_state[:I],
          init_state[:T], init_state[:L], 0.0]

    function rate_func(u, p, t)
        S, E, I, T, L, Inc = u
        N = S + E + I + T + L
        return [
            β * S * I / N,    # S → E
            ϵ * E,            # E → I
            ν * I,            # I → T
            (1 - α) * τ * T,  # T → S
            α * τ * T         # T → L
        ]
    end

    stoich = [
        -1  1  0  0  0  0;   # S → E
         0 -1  1  0  0  1;   # E → I (increments Inc)
         0  0 -1  1  0  0;   # I → T
         1  0  0 -1  0  0;   # T → S
         0  0  0 -1  1  0    # T → L
    ]

    prob = DiscreteProblem(u0, (times[1], times[end]))
    jumps = ConstantRateJump[]
    for i in 1:size(stoich, 1)
        stoich_row = stoich[i, :]
        affect! = function(integrator)
            for j in 1:length(integrator.u)
                integrator.u[j] += stoich_row[j]
            end
        end
        rate(u, p, t) = rate_func(u, p, t)[i]
        push!(jumps, ConstantRateJump(rate, affect!))
    end

    jump_prob = JumpProblem(prob, Direct(), jumps...)
    sol = solve(jump_prob, SSAStepper())

    state_names = [:S, :E, :I, :T, :L, :Inc_cumulative]
    sol_at_times = [sol(t) for t in times]
    sol_matrix = reduce(hcat, sol_at_times)'
    df = DataFrame(sol_matrix, state_names)
    df[!, :time] = collect(times)
    df[!, :Inc] = [0.0; diff(df.Inc_cumulative)]

    return df
end

@model function pmcmc_seitl(obs, init_state)
    # Priors (same as SEIT4L)
    R_0 ~ Uniform(1.0, 50.0)
    D_lat ~ Uniform(0.1, 10.0)
    D_inf ~ Uniform(0.1, 15.0)
    α ~ Uniform(0.0, 1.0)
    D_imm ~ Uniform(1.0, 50.0)
    ρ ~ Uniform(0.0, 1.0)

    θ = Dict(:R_0 => R_0, :D_lat => D_lat, :D_inf => D_inf,
             :α => α, :D_imm => D_imm, :ρ => ρ)

    times = 0.0:1.0:59.0
    traj = simulate_seitl_stochastic(θ, init_state, times)

    # Likelihood using observation vector directly
    for i in eachindex(obs)
        λ = max(ρ * traj.Inc[i], 1e-10)
        obs[i] ~ Poisson(λ)
    end
end
```

## Fit both models

```{julia}
# Initial state for SEITL (single T compartment)
init_state_seitl = Dict(
    :S => 279.0,
    :E => 0.0,
    :I => 2.0,
    :T => 3.0,
    :L => 0.0
)

# Fit SEITL - pass observations vector directly
model_seitl = pmcmc_seitl(flu_tdc.obs, init_state_seitl)
chain_seitl = sample(model_seitl, PG(20), 1000, progress=false)
```

```{julia}
# Compare the fits visually
p1 = density(chain_seitl, :R_0, title="R_0", label="SEITL")
density!(p1, chain_pmcmc, :R_0, label="SEIT4L")

p2 = density(chain_seitl, :D_imm, title="D_imm", label="SEITL")
density!(p2, chain_pmcmc, :D_imm, label="SEIT4L")

plot(p1, p2, layout=(1, 2), size=(800, 400))
```

## Posterior predictive checks

Before turning to formal model comparison criteria, it's valuable to ask a simple question: can each model generate data that looks like what we observed? This is called a *posterior predictive check*. We sample parameters from the posterior, simulate trajectories, and compare them visually to the data.

```{julia}
# Posterior predictive check for SEIT4L
function posterior_predictive_seit4l(chain, init_state, n_sims=50)
    sims = []
    n_samples = length(chain)
    indices = rand(1:n_samples, n_sims)

    for idx in indices
        θ = Dict(
            :R_0 => chain[:R_0][idx],
            :D_lat => chain[:D_lat][idx],
            :D_inf => chain[:D_inf][idx],
            :α => chain[:α][idx],
            :D_imm => chain[:D_imm][idx],
            :ρ => chain[:ρ][idx]
        )
        traj = simulate_seit4l_stochastic(θ, init_state, 0.0:1.0:59.0)
        # Apply observation model (Poisson with reporting rate)
        obs_sim = rand.(Poisson.(max.(θ[:ρ] .* traj.Inc, 1e-10)))
        push!(sims, obs_sim)
    end
    return sims
end

# Generate posterior predictive samples
ppc_seit4l = posterior_predictive_seit4l(chain_pmcmc, init_state)

# Plot
p_ppc = plot(title="SEIT4L Posterior Predictive Check",
             xlabel="Time (days)", ylabel="Daily cases")
for sim in ppc_seit4l
    plot!(p_ppc, 0:59, sim, alpha=0.2, color=:lightblue, label="")
end
scatter!(p_ppc, flu_tdc.time, flu_tdc.obs, color=:red, label="Observed", markersize=4)
plot!(p_ppc, legend=:topright)
```

```{julia}
# Posterior predictive check for SEITL
function posterior_predictive_seitl(chain, init_state, n_sims=50)
    sims = []
    n_samples = length(chain)
    indices = rand(1:n_samples, n_sims)

    for idx in indices
        θ = Dict(
            :R_0 => chain[:R_0][idx],
            :D_lat => chain[:D_lat][idx],
            :D_inf => chain[:D_inf][idx],
            :α => chain[:α][idx],
            :D_imm => chain[:D_imm][idx],
            :ρ => chain[:ρ][idx]
        )
        traj = simulate_seitl_stochastic(θ, init_state, 0.0:1.0:59.0)
        obs_sim = rand.(Poisson.(max.(θ[:ρ] .* traj.Inc, 1e-10)))
        push!(sims, obs_sim)
    end
    return sims
end

ppc_seitl = posterior_predictive_seitl(chain_seitl, init_state_seitl)

p_ppc2 = plot(title="SEITL Posterior Predictive Check",
              xlabel="Time (days)", ylabel="Daily cases")
for sim in ppc_seitl
    plot!(p_ppc2, 0:59, sim, alpha=0.2, color=:lightgreen, label="")
end
scatter!(p_ppc2, flu_tdc.time, flu_tdc.obs, color=:red, label="Observed", markersize=4)
plot!(p_ppc2, legend=:topright)
```

```{julia}
plot(p_ppc, p_ppc2, layout=(1, 2), size=(900, 400))
```

Examine these plots carefully. Both models should capture the two-wave pattern, but look for differences in how well they match the timing and magnitude of the peaks. Does one model produce simulations that more consistently match the observed data?

## Formal model comparison

::: {.callout-note title="Approaches to model comparison"}
Beyond visual checks, several quantitative approaches exist for comparing models.

**Information criteria** (DIC, WAIC, LOO-CV) estimate out-of-sample predictive accuracy. For standard MCMC output, [ParetoSmooth.jl](https://github.com/TuringLang/ParetoSmooth.jl) provides PSIS-LOO. However, applying these to particle MCMC requires care because the likelihood estimates are noisy, which can bias the results.

**Bayes factors** compare marginal likelihoods $P(\text{data}|M_1) / P(\text{data}|M_2)$. These can be estimated from particle filter output but are computationally intensive and sensitive to prior choices.

When interpreting information criteria differences: greater than 4 indicates strong evidence for the better model, 2-4 indicates moderate evidence, and less than 2 means the models are essentially equivalent.

[Camacho et al. (2011)](https://royalsocietypublishing.org/doi/10.1098/rspb.2011.0381) compared SEITL and SEIT4L using DIC and found that SEIT4L provided a better fit. This suggests that the *timing* of immunity loss matters - it's not memoryless, as the exponential distribution in SEITL would imply.
:::

::: {.callout-tip title="Learning points"}
- **Particle filters** estimate likelihoods for stochastic models by simulating many trajectories (particles) and weighting them by how well they match the data
- **Number of particles** trades off accuracy vs computation: start with 20 for exploration, use 50-100 for production
- **Stochastic vs deterministic**: stochastic models capture demographic stochasticity (important for small populations) but are much slower to fit
- **Posterior predictive checks** are a visual way to assess model fit: simulate data from the posterior and compare to observations
- **Model comparison** using information criteria helps choose between competing mechanistic explanations, though applying them to particle MCMC requires care
:::

# Going further

## Multiple chains and diagnostics

For production pMCMC, you should run multiple chains and check convergence. The pattern is:

```julia
# Run multiple chains in parallel with Particle Gibbs
chain_multi = sample(model_pmcmc, PG(50), MCMCThreads(), 2000, 4)

# Check R̂ and ESS statistics
summarystats(chain_multi)
```

Use the same diagnostics (R̂ < 1.1, ESS > 100) as for standard MCMC.

## Filtered trajectories

The Particle Gibbs sampler automatically maintains filtered trajectories (i.e. state trajectories that are consistent with the observations).
You can extract these from the chain to visualize posterior-consistent epidemic trajectories.

## Advanced particle filtering

Turing.jl's `AdvancedPS.jl` provides several particle filtering algorithms.
We used Particle Gibbs (PG), which samples both parameters and trajectories jointly and works best for complex state spaces.
An alternative is Particle Marginal Metropolis-Hastings (PMMH), which uses a bootstrap particle filter and is simpler but can suffer from degeneracy.
It's a good choice for simple models or when you don't need state trajectories.
For parameter estimation without MCMC, Sequential Monte Carlo (SMC) can be faster but requires careful tuning and is useful for quick exploratory analysis or when MCMC mixing is poor.

See the [AdvancedPS.jl documentation](https://github.com/TuringLang/AdvancedPS.jl) for details.

## SMC²

SMC² (SMC squared) uses Sequential Monte Carlo for both parameters and state trajectories, avoiding MCMC entirely.
This can be more efficient for complex models but requires more careful tuning.
Turing.jl supports this through the `SMC` sampler.

# Next session

In [Observation Models and Distance Metrics](observation_models.qmd), we'll step back and examine a fundamental question: why do we choose Poisson likelihoods, and what are the alternatives?

# References

- Andrieu et al. (2010). [Particle Markov chain Monte Carlo methods](https://rss.onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1111/j.1467-9868.2009.00736.x). *JRSS B* 72(3), 269-342. (The original PMCMC paper)
- Schön et al. (2018). [Probabilistic learning of nonlinear dynamical systems](https://arxiv.org/abs/1703.02419). *Annual Review of Control* 47, 393-404.
- Doucet et al. (2001). [Sequential Monte Carlo Methods in Practice](https://www.springer.com/gp/book/9780387951461). Springer. (Comprehensive textbook)
- Arulampalam et al. (2002). [A tutorial on particle filters](https://ieeexplore.ieee.org/document/978374). *IEEE Trans. Signal Processing* 50(2), 174-188.
- [Turing.jl documentation](https://turinglang.org/stable/) - Probabilistic programming in Julia
